name: hash browns
instructions:
shred 2 medium russet potatoes. rinse the shreds under cold water until the water runs clear (this washes off surface starch and stops them turning grey). drain, then squeeze them aggressively dry — wring them in a clean kitchen towel or spin in a salad spinner. dry potato is crispy potato; wet potato is soggy potato.

mix the dry shredded potato with 1/2 medium onion (finely chopped), 1/4 cup all-purpose flour, and 1 large egg.

heat about 1/4 inch of vegetable oil in a heavy skillet over medium-high heat. wait until the oil is sizzling — adding the potatoes to lukewarm oil is what makes them greasy and prevents the crust from forming. press the potato mixture into the pan in a 1/2-inch thick layer (one big sheet, or several pancake-sized patties).

let it cook UNDISTURBED until the bottom is deep golden brown — at least 5 minutes per side. flipping too early breaks them apart. flip, brown the other side. if cooking as one big sheet, cut into quarters before flipping for easier handling.

drain on paper towels. salt and pepper at the end (not before — salt draws water out of the raw potato and ruins the crisp).
notes:
external recipe from allrecipes ("homemade crispy hash browns"). yields 4 servings, ~35 min total.

POTATO CHOICE: russets (or idahos) for crispness — they're high-starch and produce the best crust, but can fall apart. waxy potatoes (yukon gold, red, new potatoes) hold together better but won't crisp as well. starchy is the canonical choice; use waxy if you want hash brown patties that stay intact for a sandwich.

DRYING IS THE WHOLE GAME: the single most-cited cause of soggy/falling-apart hash browns is wet potato. squeeze them in a kitchen towel or salad spinner until you genuinely cannot get any more liquid out.

BINDERS: egg + flour is the canonical binder mix here. shredded cheese (cheddar) also acts as glue when it melts and is a common community swap-in. cornstarch in place of flour is reported to give a less-mushy interior — worth trying.

DON'T SEASON THE RAW MIX WITH SALT: salt draws moisture out of raw potato. season after cooking. spices like paprika or cayenne are fine to add to the raw mix; salt specifically should wait. (note: some recipes/reviews disagree and add salt to the mix — but the dry-potato principle suggests salting after.)

OIL TEMPERATURE: oil must be sizzling hot before the potatoes go in. too-cool oil = grease-soaked hash browns, no crust.

COMMUNITY TWEAKS WORTH NOTING:
- pre-fry the onions before mixing in (drives off onion water, deepens flavor)
- shredded cheddar mixed in for flavor + structure
- garlic powder, paprika, green onions, oregano are common adds
- thinner patties cook more evenly and crisp better
- broiler finish (instead of skillet flip) for cast-iron users — cuts the breakage risk
